worth_it_explanation: User access and API credentials in the PubMatic console decide who can change pricing, inventory, and payment settings, and what an automated integration is allowed to touch. Access that drifts past what a role needs, or a shared token that never rotates, widens the surface where a mistake or a leaked credential can move revenue or expose data. This check is a review of access settings a publisher already holds in PubMatic, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a concrete account or credential the publisher can tighten.
intro: Roles and permissions decide who can act inside the PubMatic publisher console and what an automated integration can reach through the API. This subcategory covers two access surfaces. The first is human access, the user accounts and the role each one carries, which governs who can change floor pricing, inventory, demand partners, and payment details. The second is programmatic access, the API credentials and access tokens that connect reporting pulls and automated workflows to the account. These checks confirm that human roles follow least privilege and stay current with who actually works on the account, and that API credentials are scoped to what they need and rotated rather than shared and left in place.

audit_checks.title: User roles are not scoped to least privilege
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When console accounts carry broader access than the work requires, or accounts for departed staff stay active, the set of people who can change pricing, inventory, or payout settings is larger than it needs to be. That widens the surface where an accidental change or a compromised account can affect revenue or expose account data, so the gap is a standing access risk rather than a one-time event.

educate.base:
Least privilege is the practice of giving each account only the access its work requires, and no more. In a PubMatic publisher console that means a person who reads reports holds a reporting-only role rather than full administrative access, and a person who manages inventory cannot also change payment or payout settings unless that is genuinely part of their job. The principle is well established in security guidance, where access is granted as the minimum needed to complete a task rather than as a broad grant that is convenient to issue.

Two patterns pull an account away from least privilege over time. The first is access creep, where a role is granted wide to avoid a follow-up request and then never narrowed once the immediate need passes. The second is stale access, where someone leaves the team or changes roles and their console account stays active with its old permissions intact. Either pattern leaves accounts that can change pricing, inventory, demand partners, or payout details held by people who no longer need that reach.

The cost is not theoretical. An administrative account can change floor pricing, pause inventory, or alter payment settings, so each unnecessary admin account is one more place where an accidental change or a compromised login can move revenue or expose data. A departed employee's active account is the same risk with no one watching it. Tightening access does not slow the team down when the roles still match the work. It removes reach that the work no longer needs.

A few signs point to access that has drifted from least privilege:
  • Multiple accounts carry full administrative access when only one or two people actually administer the account
  • Accounts for former employees or rotated-off contractors remain active in the console
  • A person's role grants access to settings, such as payment or payout configuration, that their work does not touch
  • No record exists of who reviewed console access last or when
You may want to confirm that each console account carries the narrowest role its work requires and that no account belongs to someone who has left. One approach is to list every user with their role, confirm each against the person's current responsibilities, and remove or downgrade any account that holds more than it needs.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media operation, ad operations, editorial, and finance often touch the monetization stack for different reasons, so it helps to map each console role to the function it serves. You may want to confirm that an editorial or reporting user holds read access rather than the administrative role that ad operations needs, and that freelancers or rotated contributors do not retain active accounts after a project ends.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content operation, the people managing monetization may sit on a small team where one person wears several hats, which makes broad access feel efficient. You may want to confirm that even a small team separates the ability to change payout and payment settings from day-to-day inventory work, so a single compromised or mistaken account cannot reach both.

learn_more.source_title: Authorization Cheat Sheet (least privilege and access control)

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's detailed documentation for managing console users and assigning roles is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the list of console users with the role each one holds, then confirm each account against the person's current responsibilities and employment.

pubmatic_console.label: PubMatic publisher console
steps: Open the account administration or user-management area of the PubMatic console and list every active user with the role assigned to each.
For each user, note whether the role carries administrative access, the ability to change pricing or inventory, or access to payment and payout settings.
Confirm each account against the person's current responsibilities, and flag any role that grants more access than the work requires.
Check each account against current staff, and flag any account belonging to someone who has left the team or rotated off the engagement.
Ask the publisher when console access was last reviewed and who performed the review, and record the answer.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each console user with their role and confirms it against the person's current responsibilities and employment. An account set where every role matches the work it supports and no account belongs to a departed person passes. An account set with administrative or broad access held beyond what the work requires, or with active accounts for former staff, is a fail recorded with the specific accounts and the excess access each one holds.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each console user with their role and whether it matches their current work above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that console access at {{site_name}} is not scoped to least privilege, with accounts holding broader access than their work requires or active accounts belonging to people who have left the team. Each unnecessary administrative account is one more place where an accidental change or a compromised login can move pricing, inventory, or payout settings, so access that drifts past the work widens the risk without adding value. {{voice_recommend}} listing every console user with their role, confirming each against the person's current responsibilities, and removing or downgrading any account that holds more than its work needs. After the review, schedule a recurring access check so roles stay matched to the team as it changes.

audit_checks.title: API credentials are shared and not scoped or rotated
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An API credential that is shared across people or tools, carries broader scope than its job needs, or never rotates becomes a long-lived key with wide reach and no clear owner. If it leaks, the exposure persists until someone notices, and the shared use makes it hard to tell which integration is responsible, so the credential is a standing access risk rather than a contained one.

educate.base:
API credentials and access tokens connect automated work to a PubMatic account. A reporting pull, a scheduled data sync, or a workflow that reads or updates settings authenticates with a key or token rather than a human login. Because that credential acts on the account without a person present, how it is scoped, owned, and rotated decides how much reach it carries and how long an exposure would last.

Three patterns turn a credential into a standing risk. The first is sharing, where one key serves several people or several tools, so no single owner is accountable for it and a leak cannot be traced to one integration. The second is over-broad scope, where a credential that only needs to read reports also holds the access to change settings, so a leak reaches further than the job ever required. The third is no rotation, where a key is issued once and left in place indefinitely, so a credential that leaked months ago still works today. Security guidance treats all three as avoidable. Credentials should be tied to a known identity, scoped to the minimum the task needs, and rotated on a schedule or whenever a leak is suspected.

The fix is to give each integration its own credential, scope each one to exactly what it reads or writes, and rotate them on a defined cadence. A scoped, owned, rotated key keeps the same automation running while shrinking both the reach of any one credential and the window during which a leaked one stays useful. It also means a credential can be revoked for a single integration without breaking the others that no longer share one key.

A few signs point to API credentials that are shared or unmanaged:
  • One API key or token is reused across several people, scripts, or tools rather than each integration holding its own
  • A reporting or read-only integration authenticates with a credential that also carries write or administrative access
  • No rotation schedule exists, and credentials in use cannot be dated to when they were last issued
  • A credential has no recorded owner, so no one is accountable for revoking it if it leaks
You may want to confirm that each integration holds its own credential, scoped to what it needs and rotated on a schedule. One approach is to inventory every active key and token with its owner, scope, and age, then replace any shared, over-scoped, or stale credential with a narrowly scoped one tied to a single integration.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media operation, reporting credentials often feed dashboards or a data warehouse that several people read, which makes one shared key feel simplest. You may want to give the reporting pipeline its own read-scoped credential separate from any key that can change settings, so the widely used reporting key cannot also alter pricing or inventory if it leaks.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content operation, monetization data may flow into the same analytics stack that handles store data, so an API key can end up embedded in a shared script or service. You may want to confirm each PubMatic credential is owned by one integration and scoped to read-only where the task only reads, rather than carrying a broad key inside a script that other systems can reach.

learn_more.source_title: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet (credential scoping and rotation)

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's detailed documentation for generating and managing API credentials and access tokens is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the API credentials and access tokens in use against the account, noting the owner, the scope, and the age of each, and whether any rotation schedule exists.

pubmatic_console.label: PubMatic publisher console
steps: Open the API or developer settings area of the PubMatic console and list every active API credential or access token.
For each credential, record its owner, the integration or tool it serves, and the scope or access level it carries.
Note whether any credential is reused across more than one person or tool, which points to a shared key rather than a per-integration one.
Check whether read-only integrations authenticate with a credential that also carries write or administrative scope.
Ask the publisher whether a rotation schedule exists and when each credential was last rotated, and record the answer alongside the credential's age.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each API credential with its owner, scope, age, and whether it is shared across integrations. A credential set where each integration holds its own key, scoped to what it needs and rotated on a schedule, passes. A credential set with shared keys, over-broad scope on read-only integrations, or no rotation and undated keys is a fail recorded with the specific credentials and the issue each one carries.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each API credential with its owner, scope, and age above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that API credentials for {{site_name}} are shared across integrations, scoped more broadly than the work requires, or left in place without rotation. A shared, over-scoped, or stale credential becomes a long-lived key with wide reach and no clear owner, so a leak persists until someone notices and cannot be traced to one integration. {{voice_recommend}} inventorying every active key and token with its owner, scope, and age, giving each integration its own credential scoped to exactly what it reads or writes, and rotating them on a defined schedule. After the change, keep the inventory current so each credential stays owned, scoped, and dated.
